Practical Implementation of a Quantum String Matching
Algorithm
Simone Faro
simone.faro@unict.it
1Università di Catania
Dipartimento di Matematica e
Informatica
Catania, Italy
Francesco Pio Marino
francesco.marino@phd.unict.it
1Università di Catania
Dip. di Matematica e Informatica
Catania, Italy
2Univ Rouen Normandie, INSA Rouen
Normandie, Univ Le Havre
Normandie, Normandie Univ
LITIS UR 4108
Rouen, France
Antonio Scardace
1Università di Catania
Dipartimento di Matematica e
Informatica
Catania, Italy
ABSTRACT
String matching stands as a cornerstone problem in computer sci-
ence, finding extensive application in many fields, such as data min-
ing, text retrieval, and bioinformatics, just to cite a few. Its essence
lies in identifying all occurrences of a specified pattern, typically
denoted by length𝑚, within a text of length𝑛. Despite its seemingly
straightforward formulation, the problem has sparked a plethora of
solutions, stemming from diverse computational paradigms and ap-
proaches. Classical solutions exhibit a worst-case time complexity
of Θ(𝑛), contrasting with the capability of quantum computation to
achieve a ˜𝑂(√𝑛) complexity using Grover search, thereby realizing
a quadratic speed-up compared to classical methods. This article
presents a first practical implementation of a quantum circuit tai-
lored to address string matching, particularly focusing on binary
strings. The exposition delves into the technical intricacies of the
implementation, which leverages the Qiskit open-source toolkit
developed by IBM. By elucidating various algorithmic nuances
overlooked in prior theoretical formulations, our solution serves
as a conduit between the realms of text processing and quantum
computing, fostering cross-disciplinary dialogue and innovation.
While current real-world hardware lacks the capability to execute
the proposed circuit, particularly when dealing with lengthy texts,
this study presents experimental results from a simulation of the
circuit on classical hardware, serving to validate the efficacy of the
proposed implementation.
KEYWORDS
string matching, quantum computing, text processing, quantum
search

Simone Faro, Francesco Pio Marino, and Antonio Scardace. 2024. Practical

International Symposium on High-Performance Parallel and Distributed Com-
puting (HPDC ’24), June 3–7, 2024, Pisa, Italy. ACM, New York, NY, USA, 
8 pages. https://doi.org/10.1145/3660318.3660327
1 INTRODUCTION
Quantum computing stands at the forefront of computer science, 
employing the principles of quantum mechanics to create highly ad-
vanced computing systems that operate in fundamentally different 
ways from classical computers. Unlike classical computers, which 
rely on binary bits (0 or 1), quantum computing harnesses quantum 
bits, or qubits, which can exist in multiple states simultaneously. 
Moreover, qubits can be entangled, meaning they can perform op-
erations together, leading to faster and more efficient computations 
compared to classical bits. These unique characteristics provide 
quantum computers a significant advantage, particularly in tasks 
such as cryptography and optimization, where they demonstrate 
remarkable speed and effectiveness.
Quantum computing has led to significant breakthroughs in al-
gorithm development. Notably, Shor’s algorithm [22] for integer 
factorization and Grover’s algorithm [13] for unstructured search 
offer exponential and quadratic speed-ups over classical algorithms, 
respectively. These algorithms serve as proof-of-concept for the 
potential of quantum computing, sparking substantial interest in 
further research and development in this domain. However, it was 
the recent demonstration of quantum supremacy that ignited wide-
spread interest in quantum computing, prompting the integration of 
quantum-based solutions across various fields of computer science.
String matching has recently become a focus of interest among 
researchers in quantum theory. It’s a fundamental problem in com-
puter science [11] with applications spanning data mining, text 
retrieval, image processing, and bioinformatics. Given its broad util-
ity, string matching has emerged as an area of interest in quantum 
computation [18, 19, 21], where leveraging quantum computers 
to accelerate pattern matching algorithms could yield significant 
improvements across various domains.
Formally, given a pattern 𝑥 of length 𝑚 and a text 𝑦 of length 
𝑛, the exact string matching (ESM) problem may consists in (1) 
verifying if 𝑥 occurs in 𝑦 or in (2) finding all occurrences of 𝑥  in 𝑦 
and their corresponding positions.
17
This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution International 4.0 License.

HPDC ’24, June 3–7, 2024, Pisa, Italy Faro, et al.
Both problems (1) and (2) can be solved in Ω(𝑛) time in a classi-
cal computer [17], with an optimal average time complexity being
Θ(𝑛 log𝜎(𝑚)/𝑚) [9, 25]. Efforts to improve ESM performance in
practical scenarios have led to the development of sub-linear algo-
rithms [11]. Notably, the Boyer-Moore-Horspool algorithm [6, 15]
deserves mention for its influence on subsequent works [12].
The first quantum solution to ESM, due to Ramesh and Vinay [21],
combines Grover search [13] with a parallel string matching tech-
nique. They did not provide their solution with a quantum circuit
but rather in the quantum query complexity model and is designed
to solve problem (1) using ˜O(√𝑛) queries. Therefore, problem (2) is
solved in ˜O(√𝜌𝑛) time,1 where 𝜌 is the number of occurrences of𝑥
in 𝑦, providing real quantum advantage when 𝜌 = 𝑜(𝑛). For the av-
erage case, Montanaro [18] proved a super-polynomial separation
between quantum and classical complexity.
The first solution in the quantum circuit model of computa-
tion [25] with a ˜O(√𝑛)-time complexity for (1) was presented by
Niroula and Nam in 2021 [ 19]. The details of the corresponding
circuit were unfolded and discussed in [8]. The importance of this re-
sult is due to the fact that, perhaps, thecircuit-based quantum model
is one of the most important and most widespread quantum models,
considering that there are several programming languages charac-
terized by circuit formalism, such as IBM’s Qiskit, Microsoft’s Q#
and Google’s Cirq, just to name a few.
In this paper, we introduce the first practical implementation
of the Niroula and Nam quantum circuit, focusing on the scenario
where the strings involved are binary sequences, to make things
simpler. Assuming that the reader is familiar with the basic notions
of quantum computing, we delve into the technical intricacies of
this implementation, leveraging the open-source toolkit Qiskit,
developed by IBM and based on the Python language. Through
this study, we address algorithmic aspects overlooked in previous
theoretical formulations, thereby offering a valuable contribution
to those interested in the practical application of quantum solutions
for text processing, beyond the theoretical considerations.
The paper is structured as follows. In Section 2 we give same basic
notions while in Section 3 we provide an overview of the Niroula
and Nam algorithm. In Section 4, we detail our implementation
using Qiskit, discussing various algorithmic considerations and
presenting experimental results from circuit simulations on classical
hardware, which serve to validate the efficacy of our proposed
implementation. Finally, in Section 5, we draw our conclusions.
2 BASICS OF QUANTUM COMPUTATION
We assume in this paper that the reader has some familiarity with
quantum computing. However, to make the article self-contained,
we introduce in this section the essential notions of quantum com-
putation, useful for understanding our presentation.
Our chosen model of quantum computation in this paper is that
of reversible circuits. Quantum circuits are composed of wires car-
rying qubit values to gates executing elementary operations and
facilitate the linear movement of qubits. Input values are inscribed
onto the wires from the left side, while output values are read from
the right side. Each wire can enter at most one gate at each time
1When, in a Grover search, the number of solutions is 𝜌 > 1, and we would like to
find all of them, Θ(√𝜌𝑛) iterations are sufficient and necessary.
step. Ancillae qubits are often incorporated into the circuit defini-
tion to achieve specific computational tasks that would otherwise
be unattainable. Complexity measurement in the circuit model may
encompass the number of gates employed. However, considering
the circuit divided into discrete time-steps, where each gate appli-
cation necessitates one time-step, a more significant measure is the
circuit’s depth, indicating the total number of required time-steps.
It is noteworthy that the depth may differ from the total number of
gates since gates acting on disjoint qubits can frequently operate
in parallel. A simple yet effective example of the aforementioned
is the initialization of a quantum register of 𝑛 qubits to a quantum
state characterized by the superposition of all possible 2𝑛 values.
This initialization, achievable through 𝑛 Hadamard operators, each
acting on a different qubit, requires constant time as the Hadamard
gates are executed in parallel.
Operators in quantum computing are mathematical entities used
to represent functional processes that result in the change of the
state of a quantum register. Although there are no problems in
realizing any quantum operator capable of working in constant time
on a quantum register of fixed size, operators of variable size can
only be implemented through the composition of elementary gates.
We restrict our focus here to enumerating the gates significant to
our study. Firstly, the Pauli-X (or X or NOT) gate is the quantum
equivalent of the negation gate for classical computers with respect
to the standard basis |0⟩ and|1⟩. It operates on a single qubit,
mapping|0⟩ to|1⟩ and|1⟩ to|0⟩. The Hadamard (or H) gate is
a well-known single-qubit operation that maps the state |0⟩ to
1√
2(| 0⟩+| 1⟩) and|1⟩ to 1√
2(| 0⟩−| 1⟩), thus creating a superposition
of the two basis states with equal amplitudes. The controlled NOT
gate (or CNOT) is a quantum logic gate operating on a register of
two qubits|𝑞0, 𝑞1⟩. If the control qubit|𝑞0⟩ is set to 1, it inverts the
target qubit|𝑞1⟩; otherwise, all qubits remain the same. Formally, it
maps|𝑞0, 𝑞1⟩ to|𝑞0, 𝑞0⊕ 𝑞1⟩. The Toffoli gate (also CCNOT gate) is a
universal reversible logic gate that works on 3 qubits. If the first
two qubits are both set to 1, it inverts the third qubit; otherwise, all
bits stay the same. Formally, it maps a 3-qubit register
|𝑞0, 𝑞1, 𝑞2⟩ to
|𝑞0, 𝑞1, 𝑞0𝑞1⊕ 𝑞2⟩. The Swap gate is a two-qubit operator. Expressed
in basis states, it swaps the state of the two qubits|𝑞0, 𝑞1⟩ involved
in the operation, mapping them to|𝑞1, 𝑞0⟩.
Generalizations of the CNOT gate are the 𝑛-ary fanout operator
and the multiple-CNOT. The former uses a single control qubit and
𝑛− 1 target qubits; the latter uses 𝑛− 1 control qubits and a single
target qubit. Assuming that the target qubits are all initialized to|0⟩,
it is possible, by a divide-and-conquer strategy, to compute fanout
in depth Θ(log(𝑛)) using controlled-not gates and 0 ancillæ [10].
The most direct way to implement a multi-controlled NOT is
to use the concepts of classical Boolean logic as shown in [23] for
classical circuits, and later in [3] in the field of quantum computing.
However, this implementation requires𝑛− 2 ancillæ qubits to store
the results of partial computations. In this case the depth of the
circuit is linear with respect to the number of control qubits, since
parallelism cannot be used. In more recent papers [ 2, 14], it was
shown that a construction rearranging the circuit gates to achieve
logarithmic depth by exploiting parallelism is possible, using 𝑛− 2
and 𝑛/2 ancillæ qubits, respectively.
18
Practical Implementation of a Quantum String Matching Algorithm HPDC ’24, June 3–7, 2024, Pisa, Italy
3 QUANTUM CIRCUIT BASED ESM
In this Section we briefly outline the quantum algorithm for solving
the string matching problem as introduced in [19]. We will provide
a concise overview of the structural framework of the quantum
algorithm for solving the problem, reserving in-depth discussions
on implementation specifics for subsequent sections.
Let 𝑦 be a binary text of size 𝑛, and let 𝑥 be a binary pattern
of size 𝑚. The quantum algorithm utilizes a 4-register state|𝜓⟩ =
|𝑘⟩⊗| 𝑎⟩⊗| 𝑦⟩⊗| 𝑥⟩. The|𝑦⟩ register, with a size of 𝑛, contains the
characters of the text. Conversely, the|𝑥⟩ register, with a size of 𝑚,
contains the characters of the pattern. It’s important to emphasize
that the registers housing information pertaining to the pattern
and text are readily accessible to the circuit and do not necessitate
initialization. This method of register utilization diverges from prior
approaches to the string matching problem [21], which relied on
the quantum query model and accessed input solely through oracle
queries.
The algorithm also utilizes a register of sizelog(𝑛) for containing
a text shift value and a register|𝑎⟩ of log(𝑛) ancillary qubits. The
two registers|𝑘⟩ and|𝑎⟩ are both initialized to|0⟩⊗𝑛.
Figure 1 shows the general circuit implementation of the quan-
tum string matching. The primary operators employed within the
circuit consist of the parameterized cyclic rotation operator for a
quantum register and the match operator for two quantum registers
of equal length. These operators are depicted in Figure 1 as symbols
ROT and MATCH, respectively.
The rotation operator (denoted by the symbol ROT𝑗 ) implements
a procedure that cyclically shifts a quantum register of size 𝑛 by
𝑗 positions to the left. As a result of this rotation, the qubit at
position(𝑖+ 𝑗) mod(𝑛) of the register is moved to position 𝑖,
where 0≤ 𝑖 < 𝑛. Notably, the amplitude of the rotation applied to
the register must be specified by an input parameter stored within
the quantum register| 𝑗⟩ of size log(𝑛).
The construction of the quantum rotation register was meticu-
lously defined by Pavone and Viola in [20], who also demonstrated
that the circuit implementing the operator maintains a depth which
is at most log(𝑛). Subsequent sections will elucidate that the pa-
rameterized operator can be realized by composing at most log(𝑛)
rotation operators, resulting in a total complexity of 𝑂(log2(𝑛)).
The match operator (denoted by the symbol MATCH) embodies the
boolean oracle of the function facilitating comparison between two
binary strings of equal length
𝑛. Conceptually, the operator accepts
as input two quantum registers containing binary strings 𝑥 and 𝑦,
along with an output qubit initialized to|0⟩. Its function is to alter
the output qubit to|1⟩ only if 𝑥 = 𝑦. Symbolically it performs the
mapping MATCH|𝑥, 0⟩→| 𝑥, 𝑓(𝑥)⟩, where 𝑓(𝑥, 𝑦) = 1 ⇐⇒ 𝑥 = 𝑦.
In the ensuing sections, we will delineate how this operator can
be constructed with logarithmic depth relative to the size of the
two strings being compared.
By composing of the rotation and match operators it is possible to
construct the phase oracle of the string matching function between
a pattern 𝑥 of length 𝑚 and a text 𝑦 of length 𝑛. More formally,
such phase oracle implements the function 𝜙, built on 𝑥 and 𝑦,
such that 𝜙( 𝑗) = 1 if and only if 𝑥 = 𝑦[ 𝑗 .. 𝑗+ 𝑚.1]. The oracle is
obtained in three steps, as shown in Figure 1: (1) rotating the text
register by 𝑗 positions to the left so that the substring 𝑦[ 𝑗 .. 𝑗+ 𝑚.1]
is positioned as a prefix of 𝑦; (2) comparing the string 𝑥 with the
prefix of length 𝑚 of 𝑦; (3) rotating the text register again so that
the original configuration is re-established. The phase oracle 𝑈𝜙
performs the mapping 𝑈𝜙| 𝑗, 𝑥, 𝑦⟩→(−1) 𝜙( 𝑗)| 𝑗, 𝑥, 𝑦⟩.
Once the phase oracle of the function𝜙( 𝑗, 𝑥, 𝑦) has been defined,
it is possible to apply Grover’s search algorithm to identify one
of the possible values of
𝑗, if present, for which 𝜙( 𝑗, 𝑥, 𝑦) = 1,
answering the question that poses the problem.
It will therefore be necessary to initialize register| 𝑗⟩ to the state
|+⟩log(𝑛) by log(𝑛) Hadarmard gates and run Grover’s algorithm
for 𝜋/4√𝑛 iterations before measuring register| 𝑗⟩ to identify one
of the possible positions where 𝑥 occurs in 𝑦. We recall that each
iteration of Grover’ algorithm requires the application of the phase
oracle of the function 𝜙 and the Grover Diffuser, which represents
a rotation with respect to the state|+⟩log(𝑛).
Regarding the time complexity of the resulting algorithm, we
recall that the parameterized rotation operator requires𝑂(log2(𝑛))
time to be executed on a text register of size 𝑛, while the match
operator requires 𝑂(log(𝑚)) time to compare two strings of length
𝑚. Thus the phase oracle of the function 𝜙 can be executed in
𝑂(log2(𝑛))+ log(𝑚) = 𝑂(log2(𝑛)), since we assume 𝑚 = 𝑜(𝑛).
The Grover Diffuser requires𝑂(log log(𝑛)) time, thus each itera-
tion of the Grover’s search algorithm requires𝑂(log2(𝑛)) time. The
overall time complexity of the algorithm is therefore𝑂(√𝑛 log2(𝑛)).
Some implementation details, purely inherent to the Grover
algorithm, have been left un-outlined in the general description of
the algorithm. These include the resolution of the case in which
there are no occurrences of the pattern or the case in which there is
more than one occurrence and that number is not known a priori.
Lastly, it’s noteworthy to mention that our discussion will ex-
clude the representation of ancillary qubits within the circuits, as
illustrated in Figure 1. This decision is made for the sake of simplic-
ity, as it enables us to streamline our circuits while also aligning
with the practice in Qiskit, where the presence of ancillary qubits
is implicitly understood through the application of certain quantum
operators.
Figure 1: A general circuit implementation of the quantum
string matching algorithm [8, 19].
19
HPDC ’24, June 3–7, 2024, Pisa, Italy Faro, et al.
4 A PRACTICAL IMPLEMENTATION
In this section, we provide a comprehensive practical implemen-
tation of the ESM algorithm using Qiskit, an open-source toolkit
developed by IBM and based on the Python language. Initially, we
introduce the fundamental structure of the algorithm and the im-
plementation of the involved operators, as outlined in the reference
provided. Subsequently, we delve into more specific algorithmic
aspects that enable us to tackle the problem under broader condi-
tions. The code discussed in this study is readily accessible online
at: https://github.com/antonioscardace/Practical-Quantum-ESM
4.1 The Parameterized Cyclic Rotation Operator
The first operator whose implementation we describe is the circular
rotation operator. In this work we will use the implementation
carefully described by Pavone and Viola in [20].
A circular shift operator, denoted as 𝑅𝑠, is employed in quantum
computing to perform a shift of𝑠 positions on a register comprising
𝑛 qubits. In this work we are interested in applying left rotations to
our quantum registers, both for the pattern register and for the text
register. This operation ensures that the element at position(𝑖+ 𝑠)
mod 𝑛 is shifted to position 𝑖, implementing a circular arrangement
for elements beyond the register size 𝑛. Formally, the action of the
operator 𝑅𝑠 is represented by the permutation:
|𝑞0, 𝑞1, . . . , 𝑞𝑛−1⟩→| 𝑞𝑠, 𝑞𝑠+1, . . . , 𝑞𝑛−1, 𝑞0, 𝑞2, . . . , 𝑞𝑠−1⟩
For example, rotating a quantum register representing the state
|10110001⟩ 3 positions to the left would obtain the new configura-
tion|10001101⟩.
In quantum computing, a rotation operation of a register can
be viewed as a permutation of its elements, allowing it to be de-
composed into a series of Swap operations. This decomposition
typically requires at most
𝑛 Swaps to transform a sequence into
the desired permutation. However, within a quantum circuit, the
application of multiple Swap operators can occur concurrently,
particularly when dealing with mutually disjoint pairs of qubits.
For a register comprising 𝑛 qubits, it becomes feasible to execute
up to 𝑛/2 Swap operations simultaneously. This parallel execution
facilitates the efficient movement of 𝑛/2 qubits to their final posi-
tions within a single time step. The optimal scenario arises when
the register requires rotation precisely by 𝑛/2 positions, necessitat-
ing only 𝑛/2 parallel Swaps. Specifically, this entails exchanging
𝑞𝑖 with 𝑞𝑛/2+𝑖, for 𝑖 = 0..𝑛/2− 1. Such an operation exhibits a
time complexity of O(1), signifying constant-time execution and
exemplifying the efficiency achievable within quantum circuits.
Pavone and Viola present in their work [20] a comprehensive
procedure for constructing the circular rotation operator for reg-
isters of any size
𝑛 and any shift amount 𝑠, where both 𝑛 and 𝑠
are powers of 2. They prove that the resulting circuit exhibits a
depth of 𝑂(log(𝑛)). Although we refrain from providing a formal
proof of this assertion in our current study, it is evident that, in
the worst-case scenario, employing 𝑛/2 swap operations effectively
positions a maximum of 𝑛/2 elements, thus leaving the remaining
𝑛/2 elements for subsequent positioning. With each subsequent
step, the number of qubits requiring accurate positioning decreases
by half, enabling a complete rotation in 𝑂(log(𝑛)) steps.
def rot(n, s):
qc = QuantumCircuit(n)
for i in range(1, log2(n)-log2(s)+2):
for j in range(0, n/(s*(2**i))):
for q in range(j*s*2**i,s*(j*2**i+1)):
qc.swap(n-1-(q+s), n-1-(q+2**(i-1)*s+s))
return qc.to_gate(label=’ROT’+str(s))
def rot_gate(n,s):
return rot(n,s).to_gate(label=’Rot’+str(s))
rot(8,1) rot(8,2) rot(8,4)
Figure 2: On top: the Qiskit procedure rot(n,s) for imple-
menting the left-rotation quantum operator which cyclically
rotate a register with size n of s positions to the right. On bot-
tom: three leftward rotation operators generated by the pro-
cedure for a quantum register of 8 qubits and shift amounts
equal to 1, 2 and 4 positions, respectively.
Figure 2 shows the procedure rot(n,s) for the construction of the
left rotation quantum operator together with the circuits gener-
ated by the procedure for a quantum register of 8 qubits and shift
quantities equal to 1, 2 and 4 positions respectively .
In our implementation we need to apply, in superposition, all
possible rotations to the quantum register of size 𝑛 representing
the text. Thus the circular shift operator needs to apply a rotation
dependent on an input parameter 𝑗. In this context, the operation
involves two quantum registers within the circuit: the first register
is the parameter| 𝑗⟩, which stores the input value related to the
rotation amount, with a size of ⌈log(𝑛)⌉; the second register|𝑞⟩,
of size 𝑛, represents the register to be rotated. The initialization of
| 𝑗⟩ is expressed as| 𝑗⟩ = Ë⌊log(𝑛)⌋− 1
𝑖=0 | 𝑗𝑖⟩, where| 𝑗𝑖⟩ is initialized
with the 𝑖-th least significant bit of the binary representation of 𝑘.
Figure 3 showcases the procedural framework, prot(n), respon-
sible for constructing the parameterized rotation circuit. In the
middle, a circuit diagram instantiated by this procedure, exem-
plified for
n = 8 , is depicted. Notably, the circuit initializes the
parameter k using Hadamard operators, thereby representing all
feasible shift values between 0 and n− 1, in superposition.
For any given value of𝑖, such that 0≤ 𝑖 < log(𝑛), the application
of the rotation operator ROT2𝑖 to register|𝑞⟩ is controlled by the
qubit| 𝑗𝑖⟩. It is noteworthy that the rotation operator ROT2𝑖 can be
constructed using a circuit with a depth which is, at most, log(𝑛).
We observe that the possibility that such an operator is controlled
by a single qubit, i.e.| 𝑗𝑖⟩, is realized through the application of a
technique capable of creating multiple copies of the control qubit
and which involves the use of log(𝑛) ancillary qubits (these ancillæ
qubits are implicit in the Qiskit implementation).
20
Practical Implementation of a Quantum String Matching Algorithm HPDC ’24, June 3–7, 2024, Pisa, Italy
def parametrized_rot(n):
logn = int(np.log2(n))
jr = QuantumRegister(logn,’j’)
yr = QuantumRegister(n,’y’)
qc = QuantumCircuit(jr,yr)
for i in range(logn):
crot = rot_gate(n,2**i).control(1)
qc = qc.compose(crot, [jr[i]]+yr[:])
return qc
Figure 3: The parameterized cyclic rotation operator. The
Qiskit code useful for building the quantum circuit for a
string of length n is shown above. In the middle, the circuit
generated by the procedure for a string of lengthn=8 is shown.
Below, the results of 1000 runs of the circuit using the super-
imposed rotation parameter are shown.
On the bottom of Figure 3, we show measurements obtained
from 1000 runs of the circuit delineated on the left. It’s discernible
that the resultant alternatives from the measurements correspond
to all configurations of the rotated input string 10110001, each
bearing an equiprobable likelihood of measurement.
In summary, the parameterized leftward circular shift operator,
contingent on the value of an input quantum quantum 𝑘, utilizes
𝑛+⌈log(𝑛)⌉− 1 input qubits, along with⌈log(𝑛)⌉− 1 ancillæqubits,
exhibiting a depth equal to 𝑂(log2(𝑛)).
4.2 The Match Operator
Once the rotation operator is implemented, the next step involves
crafting the binary string matching operator for strings of length n.
In quantum computing, given a function 𝑓 :{0, 1}𝑛→{ 0, 1}, any
quantum operator that maps a register containing the value of a
given input 𝑥∈{ 0, 1}𝑛 to a register whose value depends on 𝑓(𝑥)
is called a quantum oracle. Here we are interested in a special kind
of quantum oracle, named Boolean oracle, which maps the state
register|𝑥, 0⟩, to the state register|𝑥, 𝑓(𝑥)⟩, for any 𝑥∈{ 0, 1}𝑛.
Formally, the match quantum operator serves as a Boolean Oracle
for the function 𝑓 :{0, 1}𝑛×{ 0, 1}𝑛 →{ 0, 1}, defined, for any
𝑥, 𝑦∈{ 0, 1}𝑛 as follows
𝑓(𝑥, 𝑦) =
 1 if 𝑥𝑖 = 𝑦𝑖,∀ 0≤ 𝑖 < 𝑛
0 otherwise
Given that the comparison between the 𝑛 different characters
of the two strings occurs independently, it lends itself to a circuit
of constant depth. However, the validation process to determine
whether all characters of the two strings match necessitates the
application of a multiple-CNOT operator. This operator can be im-
plemented in logarithmic time relative to the size of the two strings,
albeit requiring the utilization of a linear number of ancillary qubits.
The operator resulting from the Qiskit implementation is real-
ized through a circuit of size 2n+1, assuming that the first n qubits
hold the configuration of the first string x, while the subsequent n
qubits contain the configuration of the second string y. The final
qubit in the circuit serves as the output qubit of the Boolean oracle,
def match(n):
xr = QuantumRegister(n,’x’)
yr = QuantumRegister(n,’y’)
out = QuantumRegister(1,’out’)
qc = QuantumCircuit(xr,yr,out)
for i in range(n):
qc.cx(xr[i],yr[i])
qc.x(yr[i])
qc.mcx(yr[:],out)
for i in range(n):
qc.x(yr[i])
qc.cx(xr[i],yr[i])
return qc
def match_gate(n):
return match(n).to_gate(label=’Match’+str(n))
Figure 4: The match operator between binary strings. The
Qiskit code useful for building the quantum circuit for
strings of length n is shown above. Below, the circuit gener-
ated by the procedure for n=4 is shown.

HPDC ’24, June 3–7, 2024, Pisa, Italy Faro, et al.
Figure 5: The results of 1000 executions of the match operator
using the string y=1011 and superimposing the string x.
which will hold the comparison results—yielding 1 if x=y and 0
otherwise.
Figure 4 portrays the Qiskit procedure (at the top) responsible
for constructing the aforementioned circuit. The lower section of
the same figure illustrates the circuit for n=4.
The comparison between characters is based on the following
logical relationship, for 0≤ 𝑖 < 𝑛
𝑥𝑖 = 𝑦𝑖 ⇐⇒ NOT(XOR(𝑥𝑖, 𝑦𝑖))
Thus, character comparison is accomplished through a NOT and
a CNOT operator (to implement the XOR), while equality between
the two strings is verified using a Multiple-CNOT operator. Notably,
at the conclusion of the computation, the string registers revert to
their initial values via an uncompute process. The entire circuit
maintains a logarithmic depth in terms of n.
In Figure 5 the outcomes of 1000 executions of the circuit are
showcased, detailing the comparison between a string y=1011 of
length 4 and a string x of equivalent length. Notably, x encom-
passes, in superposition, all 16 possible binary strings. From the
computation outputs, it becomes evident that only when x=1011 is
a positive result achieved in the comparison. As anticipated, in all
other cases, the comparison yields a resolute 0.
4.3 The Exact String Matching Oracle
We are now poised to amalgamate the operators generated thus
far to construct the boolean oracle for the pattern matching op-
erator between pattern and text. The operator fashioned by the
esmo(m,n) procedure, as depicted in Figure 6, materializes in the
form of a Boolean oracle that, post-computation, deposits the out-
come within an output register. In the lower part of Figure 6, the
Boolean Oracle constructed for a pattern of length 2 and a text
of length 4 is displayed. The circuit requires the presence of an
additional register: the j register to define the amplitude of the rota-
tion of y. Notably, the uncompute process is executed by reversing
the rotation operator, adept at restoring the string y to its initial
configuration.
def esmo(m,n):
logn = int(np.log2(n))
jr = QuantumRegister(logn,’j’)
xr = QuantumRegister(m,’x’)
yr = QuantumRegister(n,’y’)
out = QuantumRegister(1,’out’)
qc = QuantumCircuit(jr,out,xr,yr)
qc = qc.compose(parametrized_rot(n), jr[:]+yr[:])
qc = qc.compose(match_gate(m), xr[:]+yr[:m]+out[:])
qc = qc.compose(parametrized_rot(n).inverse(), jr[:]+yr[:])
return qc
Figure 6: The Boolean oracle for the match between a pattern
x of length m and a text y of length n. The Qiskit code useful
for building the quantum circuit is shown above. The circuit
generated by the procedure for m=2 and n=4 is shown below.
Assuming that register| 𝑗⟩ contains the magnitude of the shift
of the text, the exact string matching boolean oracle works in four
phases: (1) applies a rotation of 𝑗 positions to the left to the register
|𝑦⟩ containing the text, through the application of the parameterized
rotation operator; (2) apply the match operator to compare the
register|𝑥⟩ with the prefix of length 𝑚 of the text register |𝑦⟩
and deposit the final result of the output qubit; (3) applies the
inverse rotation operator to return the text register|𝑦⟩ to its initial
configuration.
Since the parameterized rotation operator takes𝑂(log2(𝑛)) time
and the match operator takes 𝑂(log(𝑚)) time, the overall com-
plexity of the Boolean oracle for exact strung matching is equal to
𝑂(log2(𝑛)).
In what follows, we indicate the gate that identifies the circuit
just discussed through the symbol ESMO.
4.4 Search for a Fixed Number of Occurrences
In this section we define the circuit for solving the string matching
problem in the case in which the number of occurrences of the
pattern within the text is known a priori. In their solution Niroula
and Nam [19] identify one of the occurrences of the pattern using
Grover’s search algorithm [13].
Grover’s search algorithm is one of the most famous results
proving theoretical quantum supremacy. It searches a desired item
within an unstructured dataset of 𝑛 items in 𝑂(√𝑛) time, which
is quadratically lower than the lower-bound to the runtime of an
algorithm solving the same problem in a classical model of com-
putation. The algorithm is inherently bounded-error, but we know
22
Practical Implementation of a Quantum String Matching Algorithm HPDC ’24, June 3–7, 2024, Pisa, Italy
def GroverESM(ESMO,m,n,t):
logn = int(np.log2(n))
jr = QuantumRegister(logn,’j’)
xr = QuantumRegister(m,’x’)
yr = QuantumRegister(n,’y’)
out = QuantumRegister(2,’out’)
cr = ClassicalRegister(logn+1,’c’)
qc = QuantumCircuit(jr,xr,yr,out,cr)
qc.h(jr)
qc.x(out[0])
qc.h(out[0])
iterations = int((np.pi/4)*(np.sqrt(n/t)))
for i in range(iterations):
qc = qc.compose(ESMO, jr[:]+xr[:]+yr[:]+out[0])
qc = qc.compose(Diff, jr[:])
qc.measure(jr, cr[:logn])
qc = qc.compose(ESMO, jr[:]+xr[:]+yr[:]+out[1])
qc.measure(out[1], cr[logn])
return qc
Figure 7: Qiskit procedure for building the Grover’s search
algorithm for identifying t occurrence of a patternx of length
m within a text y of length n. The implementation assumes
that both strings are binary sequences and that the number
t of occurrences of x in y is known.
there is no quantum algorithm with less than 𝑛 queries that solves
the problem with certainty for an arbitrary dataset [4].
Given a function 𝑓 :{0, 1}log(𝑛)→{ 0, 1} with a unique solution
𝑤∈{ 0, 1}log(𝑛) such that 𝑓(𝑤) = 1, the algorithm uses a rotation
of the quantum register representing the superposition of all inputs
𝑗∈{ 0, 1}log(𝑛) to increase the probability of getting the desired
solution 𝑤 when measuring the register.
Figure 7 shows the Qiskit procedure for building the Grover’s
algorithm for searching a pattern of length m in a text of length m,
assuming that the number t of occurrences is known.
More in detail, the algorithm starts with a register encoding
the uniform superposition of all possible inputs 𝑗 ∈{ 0, 1}log(𝑛).
Note that the objective register, in which the unique solution 𝑤
has amplitude 1 and all other items have amplitudes 0, is nearly
orthogonal to the starting register. Thus the goal of the algorithm
is to apply to the initial register a rotation as close as possible to
𝜋/2 to bring it closer to the objective register. A single iterative
step of the algorithm consists of two rotations.
The first one consists in a phase oracle gate for the function 𝑓 ,
which flips the amplitude of the qubit |𝑤⟩ corresponding to the
searched item, leaving the amplitudes of all other inputs unchanged.
Such phase oracle is obtained by initializing to the state |−⟩ the
output qubit of the ESMO boolean oracle.
The second rotation is performed by the Grover’s Diffusion op-
erator (identifyied by symbol Diff), which operates a reflection
across|+⟩⊕𝑛. The overall rotation performed during a single Grover
iteration is approximately equal to 2/√𝑛 radiants. Thus, 𝜋/4√𝑛 it-
erations are necessary and sufficient.
In the case where the function 𝑓 has exactly 𝑡 solutions, with
𝑡 > 1, 𝑂(√𝑛) iterations are still enough to find a solution, but it can
be shown that roughly 𝜋
4
√︃
𝑛
𝑟 iterations are required [7], and that
this bound is optimal [5].
The circuit constructed by the procedure shown in Figure 7, after
having applied the Grover’s algorithm to search for the text shift and
having measured the
| 𝑗⟩ register, applies a verification procedure by
executing the ESMO boolean oracle once and depositing the result in
a second output register (out[1]). Formally the output value will
be equal to|1⟩ if and only if 𝑥 = 𝑦[ 𝑗 ... 𝑗+ 𝑚− 1].
Figure 8 shows the Grover’s circuit for searching a pattern x
= 00 in a text y = 11010011 , with a single occurrence. In this
case, Grover’s algorithm carries out 2 iterations to converge the
initial state|+⟩ to the vector|𝑤⟩. The plot below shows the result
of 1000 runs of the circuit, highlighting how the correct solution is
identified with a probability greater than 96%.

def search(x, y)
m = len(x)
n = len(y)
for t in range(1, int(n/2)+1):
results = run(GroverESM(x, y, t), 1)
results = list(results.keys())[0]
outcome = int(results[0])
position = int(results[1:], 2)
if outcome:
return position

method, which calls adaptevely Grover’s algorithm.
4.5 Solving the General Case
In the general case, the value 𝑡 of occurrences is not known a priori
and can only be obtained through a complex procedure based on
the Quantum Phase Estimation [16]. Lacking this information pre-
cludes the application of Grover’s search algorithm, as the number
of iterations hinges solely on its awareness. Since this algorithm
works even if a correct 𝑡∗ estimate of 𝑡 is known, up to a small
multiplicative error 1+ 𝜀, such that 1/(1+ 𝜀)≤ 𝑡/𝑡∗≤ 1+ 𝜀, several
approximate counting solutions have also been proposed [1, 5].
An effective practical solution for this problem has been recently
given by Venkateswaran and O’Donnell [24]. It consists in adap-
tively estimating 𝑡 by trying all the possible values in the range
[1.1𝑖], for 1≤ 𝑖 ≤ log1.1(𝑁/2), until a solution is found or, al-
ternatively, responding negatively if no solutions are found. This
approach requires a total number of Grover iterations) which is still
𝑂(Í
𝑖
√︁
𝑁/1.1𝑖) = 𝑂(
√
𝑁). The Qiskit procedure implementing
this solution is shown in Figure 9.
For completeness, we also mention a strategy with improved
query complexity for large 𝑡, which was given by Boyer, Brassard,
Høyer, and Tapp [5]. In brief, for𝑖 = 0, 1, 2, . . . , 𝑁/2 it tries a random
number of iterations in the range[1, 1.1𝑖], stopping if ever a solution
is found. This algorithm solves the search problem using 𝑂(
√︁

iterations, despite not knowing 𝑡 in advance.
5 CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORKS
The primary objective of this study is to offer a comprehensive
elucidation of the Exact String Matching (ESM) problem within
the realm of Quantum Computation. We present an exhaustive
exposition of the requisite operators, gates, and the Boolean Oracle
involved in the computational circuitry. Subsequently, we delineate
two distinct implementations of the ESM problem: the former pre-
supposes a priori knowledge of the number of solutions, whereas
the latter dynamically endeavors to estimate the solution count.
In future research, we aspire to explore alternative String Match-
ing algorithms well-established in classical computation, such as pa-
rameterized and approximate algorithms. Additionally, another in-
triguing avenue for exploration would involve investigating method-
ologies for adaptively estimating the number of solutions.
